The person who sins will die. Regarding eternity, every person was, is, and will be treated according to how their actions show them to have been under the old agreement of works or the new agreement of grace. Whatever outward suffering comes upon people through the sins of others, they deserve for their own sins all that they suffer; and the Lord directs every event for the eternal good of believers. All souls are in the hand of the great Creator: he will deal with them in justice or mercy; nor will anyone perish because of another’s sins, who is not in some sense worthy of death for his own. We all have sinned, and our souls must be lost if God deals with us according to his holy law; but we are invited to come to Christ. If a person who had shown their faith by their actions had a wicked son, whose character and conduct were the opposite of their parent's, could it be expected he should escape divine punishment because of his father’s piety? Surely not. And should a wicked man have a son who walked before God as righteous, this man would not perish for his father’s sins. If the son was not free from evils in this life, still he should share in salvation. The question here is not about the meritorious basis of justification, but about the Lord's dealings with the righteous and the wicked.

The wicked person will be saved if they turn from their evil ways. A true penitent is a true believer. None of their former wrongdoings will be mentioned to them, but in the righteousness that they have done as the result of faith and the effect of conversion, they will surely live. The question is not whether truly righteous people ever become apostates. It is certain that many who for a time were thought to be righteous do so, while speaks the fullness of pardoning mercy: when sin is forgiven, it is blotted out; it is remembered no more. In their righteousness they will live; not for their righteousness, as if that were an atonement for their sins, but in their righteousness, which is one of the blessings purchased by the Mediator. What encouragement a repenting, returning sinner has to hope for pardon and life according to this promise! Verse 28 shows the beginning and progress of repentance. True believers watch and pray, and continue to the end, and they are saved. In all our disagreements with God, he is in the right, and we are in the wrong.

The Lord will judge each of the Israelites according to his ways. This forms a basis for an encouragement to repent and to make a new heart and a new spirit. God does not command what cannot be done, but encourages us to do what is within our power, and to pray for what is not. Ordinances and means are appointed, directions and promises are given, that those who desire this change may seek it from God.
